sentences.text: I wanted to go back to ghetto and sneak in, and in that little town what I was in, Skala, started to be dangerous.
They were taking Jews out.
Then, I started to go to the ghetto.
I went to the ghetto.
That time, they already took out my sister with the little niece, and they killed them some place.
I don't know even where.
I didn't have an address even where to go in.
I didn't have nobody.
I didn't know where my brother-in-law is living with his daughter, the older daughter, because the younger daughter went with my sister and died there.
That was the older sister, Helen.
And people told me where my brother-in-law lives.
So I went to look for him, but they were living a few people in that room.
I didn't know what to do.
They didn't want to let me in there.
And my brother-in-law was working, so he didn't know that I came back to the ghetto, you know, to look for shelter.
What?
I was always dressed like a Polish girl because that's what I had already.
But I was looking for my brother-in-law.
He was not-- I didn't see him.
The other people living in that apartment didn't want to let me in-- was no space.
Where am I going to sleep?
So I went down and I was walking, walking, looking where to go in to sleep.
Then, my brother-in-law came back from work and he started to look for me because he heard that I am back in the ghetto.
And he found me in a Jewish spa, you know.
It was such a little bathhouse where you go in to take a bath, you know.
That was in the ghetto.
They made it.
So I went there inside-- happened to knew that woman who took care of that spa.
So she let me sit there.
I was sitting there.
I think it's supposed to be cold weather because I was sitting there warming up.
In the meantime, my brother-in-law came back and he found out that they didn't let me into the house.
And he loved me very much because he loved my sister.
Thinking about his wife, he was thinking about me, to help me.
So he was running around looking for me and people told him they saw me going in there.
He found me.
He took me home.
So I was staying for a while with them.
And then, I start at that time, I went to the office where they took people to work.
The Germans used to come every day.
In German, they call it Arbeitsamt. 'Arbeitsamt' is like here, they look for jobs.
They go, you know.
In that time, they came from Schindler people, the guy, and he took me to Schindler to work.
He chose me.
It was a lot of girls, a lot of girls.
And we were staying close, and six of us were staying.
And he chose us, and he took six girls with him to work, to Schindler.
Yes.
She was in the ghetto living with her father.
That was where I went.
Nobody let me in.
No, no, no.
My son was taken out from the ghetto when also they were taking the Jewish children, all the children they took.
And I was, at that time, in the ghetto.
I was at that time in the ghetto.
I was walking with him to a kindergarten, you know, where children were playing.
So I used to leave him there for a few hours and used to go to work to Schindler.
That was after I went to Schindler to work.
So I had to leave him in somebody's hands and I used to go to work.
But when I was in the ghetto, that was the time when the Germans came in and everybody had to go to a special place, you know, all the children, all the mothers, all the older people.
Me, they didn't take it because I was working for Schindler already.
So they didn't touch me, but they grabbed my baby away and they didn't want to let him go, you know?
How much I was screaming and yelling didn't help.
They took a lot of children, hundreds of children they took that day away.
And I don't know where they took him, or to Auschwitz or to some other place.
I didn't know.
So then, I had to work by Schindler at the [GERMAN].
What can I do?
And that time, Sam already was my boyfriend, you know, a friend.
He used to help me out.
How did he help me out?
He used to come to wash my hair.
I got very long hair, beautiful black hair.
He used to wash my hair.
He used to bring me some bread, something, you know, and he helped me out a lot.
Describe what your work at the Schindler factory was like.
When I came to Schindler's factory, I didn't know what we were going to do.
And we were waiting, six of us, shaking, scared, very much scared because we didn't know what-- what's going to be.
And all of a sudden, we saw a gorgeous man walking to us, tall, handsome-- gorgeous.
What can I tell you?
The blue eyes, beautiful eyes, and he was smiling, you know?
He was smiling.
That was Schindler.
So he came over to us close and he saw that we are shaking like that, holding each other like we would go to death.
So in German, he said-- [GERMAN].
You understand German?
No. 'Don't worry, children.'
Don't worry.
It's going to be all right.
Who's going to work for me will live through the war, you know, with such a voice like my mother says, don't worry, my child.
I'm taking care of you.
And me, I, personally, I felt good.
I believed him, you know, because his eyes were such good eyes.
And my girlfriend says to me, you believe a Nazi?
He doesn't mean what he's talking.
Who knows what he's going to do with us here?
I said, don't worry.
Don't worry.
We will see.
We will see.
There's no other way.
We had to do it.
And then, from that day, we started to go to work to him from up, not from the ghetto already, but from Plaszow .
It was a concentration camp over Krakow.
The ghetto, they liquidated.
The ghetto they liquidated.
They took us to the Plaszow, to camp Plaszow.
And from Plaszow--
I think it was the 13 of March or something.
Yeah, I think so.
I don't remember.
I think '43, yeah.
So we went to Plaszow and--
The group, the whole group went.
Sure, we walked.
We always walked.
We always walked.
Nobody took us by car.
There were no cars, no trolley cars for us, I mean.
So we went up to Plaszow.
And there, we got spaces where to sleep, where we go and sleep, like 100 women here, 100 women there.
Again, I have to tell you that was miserable.
It was just a horror.
And in the morning, the Germans used to come, the police, and they used to take us every morning to Schindler's factory to work.
There, we worked the whole day.
After the day, they used to call us and they used to take us back to Plaszow.
It was taking a long time.
A few months, we were walking.
And then Schindler got news something is going wrong.
So he build up a camp in his factory.
By his factory, he build up a camp.
Half was for men and half was for women.
And he put us in his camp.
We shouldn't walk to Plaszow every day.
And that was good.
We felt safe.
The moment we were in his hands, we felt that we are safe, and we were safe.
I'm going to go back to before you left the ghetto.
Was there any way to make sure you got onto the list that was working for Oskar Schindler?
I didn't know.
I didn't know that time about the list.
I didn't know nothing about the list.
He was making it, but we didn't know who's going to be, who's not going to be.
But I don't know.
Some crooked way, my husband was on the list.
I was on the list.
You mean from ghetto?
I think that was the [GERMAN].
It was the 13 of March, I think.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
By that time when they took us?
No, with us was no violence, nothing.
All right, if somebody walked slow or somebody stopped because, well, women, they were sick-- so they did-- you know?
We didn't know.
We didn't know.
We were guessing maybe dead, maybe-- we didn't know where the other camps-- where they take them.
I didn't know.
Nobody was talking.
In the ghetto, before the Plaszow?
No, we used to concentrate together, like a group of Jews made themselves a little steeple, you know, a little synagogue-- not 'synagogue,' a room.
They used to pray there.
We used to help how we could, you know?
Very religious people, they always found a place where to pray, always.
But if the Germans caught up, oh, they were terrible.
They didn't like that.
You know, even when you go by a plane to Israel-- now, I went hundreds of times.
I had a brother there.
So, anyway, Jews are praying in the plane wherever they can.
They're staying by the toilet.
They're staying by there, but they're praying.
When I was in the ghetto?
No, I was never sick.
I was a very healthy girl.
I was never sick.
I could take the winter.
I could take the summer.
I was walking, walking.
I was never sick.
Well, sometimes a cold, a sneeze, you know, but that was nothing.
That never stopped me from going to work.
And then, I was scared.
If I wouldn't go to work, they wouldn't let me anymore, and who knows what they would do with me.
It was never, never enough, because what we could we were grabbing, you know?
What we got, some jewelry somebody had-- if I had a golden ring or I got a bracelet leftover or something, I was exchanging for a loaf of bread or somebody.
And always black market was going on.
If you had something to sell, you could get something to eat.
Then came a time when I did not have what to give and what to sell.
And so, in ghetto, we used to have such a kitchen.
They used to give out soup.
So we went for that soup.
No.
No, I don't remember to have-- no, we didn't even have cards that we work for Schindler.
But they believed when we said [GERMAN].
The concentration camp, Plaszow?
Yeah, it was a few factories there.
Jews were working at the factories and they got food.
Every day, you could get some soup, you know, piece of bread.
But what was terrible was the hygiene was terrible.
The hygiene was just-- I couldn't take it because I got long hair, thick hair, beautiful black too, such braids.
Lice, I got lice because I couldn't--
I couldn't wash my hair every day and take care of them.
So I didn't take care of them.
They used to eat me up and not me.
Procedure-- Germans took over and they counted us.
They counted how many.
And they gave us the spots where to go in, where to sleep.
So we had our place in the barrack.
You had your place.
Whenever you came back from work, you knew this is your place where you sleep.
We were sleeping six girls on one-- on one wooden thing.
You saw it on television.
That's how it looks.
Oh, we went-- mostly, we were friends.
Mostly, we were the girls what we worked together, and we liked each other.
We helped each other.
This one was crying.
This one was yelling, so we had to go over and help and talk.
We were laughing.
I made myself to laugh.
I said, oh, don't worry, you-- you're going to live through the war, talking, you know, about unbelievable things, because who believed that this is going to be finished?
But you had to, if you wanted to help somebody.
I remember a part of it, I got a very beautiful voice.
I had a beautiful voice.
I don't know where it went, but I had.
I used to go around the girls.
I used to sing.
I used to sing.
I knew a lot of operas and they loved it.
I used to sing arias from the operas, from from-- from this, from that.
They loved that, and-- so they were happy.
You need to have the guts to make somebody happy.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nobody helped me in the meantime, just myself.
But as I told you, in the biggest trouble, in the biggest troubles what I had, I always had a smile on my face.
And people remembered me by that and they loved it, because when I came in, I was smiling.
If I smile, it must be good, you know?
It was no good.
Until now, they know me.
Oh, when I come to a place, are you always smiling?
You're always happy?
I said, I am.
When you were in Plaszow, were you aware of Jewish holidays?
Not very much.
We knew when it is.
We knew because we were counting.
We knew, but we didn't keep up Jewish holiday.
How could you keep up a Jewish holiday?
No synagogue, no dinners-- nobody cooked us a dinner to sit down.
It was not enough.
It was not enough.
If you knew somebody, they used to bring you a piece of bread, you know.
If you knew somebody, they gave you more soup.
When they were giving soup, they gave you another dish of soup.
But so it was never enough.
Toilets for women?
That was-- they made a barrack with holes.
There were a lot of holes, but a lot of women were waiting.
You couldn't even sit down and be comfortable to do what you had to do.
You couldn't because they will stink and you had to go in.
It was terrible, terrible, terrible.
I worked, I think, from 7 o'clock in the morning until 5:00 in the evening.
Yes, we had at noontime soup, a piece of meat in the soup, you know.
And then, in the evening, you got a dinner.
You got a soup and also a piece of bread.
It was [NON-ENGLISH] work, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
We used to work on the pots and pans, you know?
So I had the job to carry a big piece of wooden thing, you know, so wide and so long, here on my arm.
And I used to walk over to that thing where a man was working, was putting the white [NON-ENGLISH] on it.
'Enamel,' yeah, 'enamel.' We used to call [NON-ENGLISH].
And I used to carry that thing with six parts to the oven, where they used to bake it, you know.
And I used to leave it there, and then go back and bring again.
Go back and bring again.
That wasn't so easy.
Sure, they were heavy.
It was hard work.
There was one supervisor who was a devil.
He was the worst what can be, the worst.
We used to complain, but it didn't help us.
We used to complain to the Jewish men, but it didn't help, you know.
They were treating us sometimes terrible, you know, like we used to work and I was tired, you know.
We girls, we had menstruation, so we couldn't walk so fast, you know?
We were bothered by that.
We had pains here, so we walk slowly.
Well, they would knock over the back, you know, to walk faster, faster.
But Schindler was watching from the window of his office.
He was staying and looking down.
He didn't like something like that.
That's why we felt safe because we knew that he's watching us.
He was watching.
And she used to come to the factory to help out, his wife.
She used to come.
She didn't like to come because he always had a lover, always had a woman, lover, and she didn't like that.
But when she was, she went to the kitchen.
She used to cook up a storm, a lot of meat.
She was so good to us.
She was like a mother, wonderful.
Once, she passed me by.
She got a baked potato.
She held a baked potato and the baked potato was hot.
I don't know how she hold it.
And she saw me.
I was walking too.
She took the potato and threw it on me, you know?
So I catched it, and that was hot.
I picked up my [GERMAN] and holding that [GERMAN].
And this I remember, and this I remind her when she was in New York.
She came to New York, you know.
I'm grabbing thing over thing.
We honored her in that big hotel in New York.
What is the name?
Plaza?
No.
It was a Jewish organization, a very religious Jewish organization who's all around the world.
They call it Aish HaTorah, Aish HaTorah.
That was the office in Los Angeles-- very charitable, big.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Oh, Plaszow was run by a crazy guy, Goth, Goth.
He was in the picture, that fat guy, Goth.
Oh, he was a devil, a murderer and devil.
And my husband was left in Plaszow because he didn't come down with me to Schindler, you know?
So he was left there.
So I got news from him through some policeman that he's in terrible danger.
He's afraid they're going to kill him there.
How long were you in Plaszow before you went to the Schindler barracks?
I was working from Plaszow by Schindler.
I was a few months.
Oh, I witnessed-- I witnessed he was playing around with people.
He was staying on his balcony shooting people and nothing-- people didn't do nothing to him.
They passed by.
A woman passed by.
He liked that.
She walked-- boom, put her down.
He was crazy, and that's why he got scared, Sam, that they're going to kill him, too.
So he let me know when I was already in Emalia that he-- I should help him.
So how could I help him?
All of a sudden, I got crazy also, you know.
Everybody was crazy in the war, doing things unbelievable.
So I got the same person.
